steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: The blank fills the word record, completing the noun phrase fossil record. Once that word is in place, the left side becomes a complete independent clause: they found something never before seen in the fossil record. What follows the colon is an appositive — evidence of cloning — that renames or elaborates on what they found. After the period begins a new independent clause starting with In clonal fragmentation.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
A colon introduces an elaboration after a complete clause; a period separates independent clauses. The punctuation “record: evidence of cloning. In” applies both correctly: the colon after record introduces the appositive evidence of cloning, and the period launches the new clause In clonal fragmentation…

  • "record. Evidence of cloning in": leaves Evidence of cloning in... without a main verb and runs it into the following independent clause, an animal splits.... ✗
  • "record—evidence of cloning in": the dash introduces an appositive, but the sentence still fails to separate that appositive from the following independent clause. ✗
  • "record, evidence of cloning, in": the commas create a run-on structure when the phrase leads into the independent clause an animal splits itself in two. ✗
  • "record: evidence of cloning. In": colon after record + period before the new clause — both marks are correctly placed. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
